---
title: "U.S. State Finance Dashboard"
format:
  dashboard:
    orientation: rows
    theme: cosmo
execute:
  echo: false
---


```{python}
#| include: false

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from itables import show

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv("data/finance.csv")

# Fix column names
df.columns = (
    df.columns
    .str.strip()
    .str.replace(r"\.\s+", ".", regex=True)
)

# Clean columns
columns_to_numeric = [
    "Year",
    "Totals.Revenue",
    "Totals.Expenditure",
    "Totals.Tax",
    "Totals.Intergovernmental",
    "Details.Education.Education Total",
    "Details.Health.Health Total Expenditure",
    "Details.Transportation.Highways.Highways Total Expenditure",
    "Details.Welfare.Welfare Institution Total Expenditure",
    "Totals.Debt at end of fiscal year"
]

for col in columns_to_numeric:
    df[col] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors="coerce")

# Remove incomplete rows

df = df.dropna(subset=["State", "Year", "Totals.Revenue", "Totals.Expenditure"])
df["State"] = df["State"].str.upper()

# Create calculated columns

df["Budget Balance"] = df["Totals.Revenue"] - df["Totals.Expenditure"]

df["Debt Ratio"] = df["Totals.Debt at end of fiscal year"].div(df["Totals.Revenue"]).mul(100)

df["Education Share"] = df["Details.Education.Education Total"].div(df["Totals.Expenditure"]).mul(100)

df["Budget Status"] = df["Budget Balance"].apply(lambda x: "Surplus" if x >= 0 else "Deficit")


# Filters

selected_year = 2019
selected_state = "CALIFORNIA"

# Filtered data
df_year = df[df["Year"] == selected_year].copy()
state_data = df[df["State"] == selected_state].copy().sort_values("Year")
state_year_data = df_year[df_year["State"] == selected_state].copy()


# KPI values
avg_revenue = df_year["Totals.Revenue"].mean()
avg_expenditure = df_year["Totals.Expenditure"].mean()
avg_debt_ratio = df_year["Debt Ratio"].mean()
state_budget_balance = state_year_data["Budget Balance"].iloc[0] if not state_year_data.empty else None

balance_color = "success" if state_budget_balance is not None and state_budget_balance >= 0 else "danger"

# Figure 1: Top 10 states by expenditure
top10_expenditure = df_year.sort_values("Totals.Expenditure", ascending=False).head(10)

fig_top_expenditure = px.bar(
    top10_expenditure,
    x="Totals.Expenditure",
    y="State",
    orientation="h",
    color="Budget Status",
    labels={"Totals.Expenditure": "Total Expenditure ($)", "State": "State"},
    title=f"Top 10 States by Total Expenditure ({selected_year})"
).update_yaxes(categoryorder='total ascending')


# Figure 2: Revenue vs debt
df_debt = df_year.dropna(subset=["Totals.Debt at end of fiscal year"])

fig_revenue_vs_debt = px.scatter(
    df_debt,
    x="Totals.Revenue",
    y="Totals.Debt at end of fiscal year",
    hover_name="State",
    color="Budget Status",
    labels={
        "Totals.Revenue": "Total Revenue ($)",
        "Totals.Debt at end of fiscal year": "Total Debt ($)"
    },
    title=f"Revenue vs Debt ({selected_year})"
)


# Figure 3: Revenue vs expenditure trend for selected state
fig_trend_state = px.line(
    state_data,
    x="Year",
    y=["Totals.Revenue", "Totals.Expenditure"],
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Bold,
    title=f"Revenue vs Expenditure Trend for {selected_state}"
)
   

# Figure 4: Spending breakdown for selected state
selected_row = state_year_data.iloc[0]

sector_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Sector": ["Education", "Health", "Transportation", "Welfare"],
    "Value": [
        selected_row["Details.Education.Education Total"],
        selected_row["Details.Health.Health Total Expenditure"],
        selected_row["Details.Transportation.Highways.Highways Total Expenditure"],
        selected_row["Details.Welfare.Welfare Institution Total Expenditure"]
        ]
    })

fig_spending_breakdown = px.bar(
        sector_df,
        x="Sector",
        y="Value",
       color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Bold,
        labels={"Value": "Expenditure ($)", "Sector": "Spending Sector"},
        title=f"Spending Breakdown in {selected_state} ({selected_year})"
).update_layout(coloraxis_showscale=False)


# Figure 5: Highlight selected state
df_year["Highlight"] = df_year["State"].apply(
    lambda x: selected_state if x == selected_state else "Other States"
)

fig_state_highlight = px.scatter(
    df_year,
    x="Totals.Revenue",
    y="Totals.Expenditure",
    hover_name="State",
    color="Highlight",
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Bold,
    labels={
        "Totals.Revenue": "Total Revenue ($)",
        "Totals.Expenditure": "Total Expenditure ($)",
        "Highlight": "State"
    },
    title=f"Revenue vs Expenditure with {selected_state} Highlighted ({selected_year})"
)


# Insight values
max_education_state = df_year.loc[df_year["Details.Education.Education Total"].dropna().idxmax(), "State"]

max_health_state = df_year.loc[df_year["Details.Health.Health Total Expenditure"].dropna().idxmax(), "State"]


max_debt_state = df_year.loc[df_year["Debt Ratio"].dropna().idxmax(), "State"]
   
```


# OVERVIEW
The key indicators summarize the average financial performance across states and highlight the selected state’s budget position.

## Row {height="20%"}

::: {.valuebox icon="cash" color="info" title="Average Revenue"}
`{python} f"${avg_revenue:,.0f}"`
:::

::: {.valuebox icon="credit-card" color="primary" title="Average Expenditure"}
`{python} f"${avg_expenditure:,.0f}"`
:::

::: {.valuebox icon="percent" color="teal" title="Average Debt Ratio"}
`{python} f"{avg_debt_ratio:.1f}%"`
:::

::: {.valuebox icon="bank" color="`{python} balance_color`" title="Budget Balance (California)"}
`{python} f"${state_budget_balance:,.0f}" if state_budget_balance is not None else "No data"`
:::

## Row {height="80%"}

### Column {width="50%"}

```{python}
fig_top_expenditure
```

States at the top of the chart allocate significantly more resources, indicating larger government operations and higher public spending.


### Column {width="50%"}

```{python}
fig_revenue_vs_debt
```

This chart illustrates the relationship between state revenue and total debt.
States with higher revenue often carry higher debt levels, reflecting larger economies and greater capacity to borrow.
However, states with relatively high debt compared to their revenue may face greater financial risk.


# STATE TRENDS

## Row

### Column {width="50%"}

```{python}
fig_trend_state
```

This chart shows how revenue and expenditure changed over time for **`{python} selected_state`**. 


### Column {width="50%"}

```{python}
fig_state_highlight
```

Insight

This chart shows how revenue and expenditure changed over time for **`{python} selected_year`**.

This chart compares **`{python} selected_state`** with other states in **`{python} selected_year`**. Its position indicates whether it operates at a larger or smaller financial scale relative to others.

The relationship between revenue and expenditure also provides insight into whether the state maintains a balanced budget or shows signs of deficit spending.


# SELECTED STATE SPENDING

## {height="85%"}

```{python}
fig_spending_breakdown
```

## {height="15%"}

This chart shows how the selected state allocates its spending across key sectors such as Education, Health, Transportation, and Welfare.
The distribution highlights the state’s policy priorities and areas of investment.



# ABOUT

This dashboard explores U.S. state government finances across revenue, expenditure, debt, and sector spending. It compares states in a selected year and also shows the trend over time for one selected state.

The data used in this dashboard comes from the CORGIS Finance Dataset.

Source: CORGIS Dataset Project  
Dataset: finance.csv

```{python}
show(
    df,
    caption="Full Finance Dataset",
    buttons=["csvHtml5"]
)
```
